title: OAuth 2.0 — Rich Authorization Requests (RAR)
description: Rich Authorization Requests (RFC 9396) replace coarse-grained scopes with a structured authorization_details parameter that precisely describes what the client is authorized to do. Instead of scope=payments (which could mean 'make any payment'), the client specifies the exact payment type, amount, currency, and recipient. The AS embeds the granted authorization_details in the access token, and the resource server enforces the specific constraints — not just 'can make payments' but 'can make exactly this payment'.

steps.label: Initiate payment of €250

artifacts.raw_input: { amount: 250.00 EUR, recipient: John Smith, iban: DE02100100109307118603 }
artifacts.signing_key_hint: Client builds authorization_details JSON from the payment intent

steps.label: 1. Why scopes are not enough
steps.value:
Traditional scope-based authorization for payments:

  scope = "payments:write"

This tells the AS: "the client wants to make payments."
It tells the RS nothing useful:
  • How much? (could be any amount)
  • To whom? (could be any recipient)
  • Which account? (not specified)
  • One payment or unlimited? (not specified)

The RS must trust the client to stay within bounds — or
implement its own out-of-band authorization system.
Scope is a permission category, not a permission contract.

steps.label: 2. authorization_details — the structured alternative
3. Type registry and extensibility
steps.value:
The "type" field is the key to RAR extensibility:

  "payment_initiation"  → Open Banking / PSD2 payments
  "openid_credential"   → OpenID for Verifiable Credentials
  "account_information" → Open Banking account read
  "medical_record"      → FHIR-based health data access
  "document_access"     → specific document + permissions

Each type defines its own schema. The AS understands
the type to show an appropriate consent UI.
The RS understands the type to enforce the exact
operation that was authorized.

authorization_details can coexist with scope in the same
request — they are additive, not mutually exclusive.

steps.explanation: The user initiates a €250 payment. The client builds a structured authorization_details JSON describing the exact payment operation and redirects the browser to the AS with it encoded in the authorization request. This is the key difference from scope-based authorization — the intent is fully specified upfront.
annotations: RFC 9396 §2: authorization_details is a JSON array encoded as a query parameter (URL-encoded JSON string).

For large authorization_details payloads, PAR (RFC 9126) should be used to push the request server-to-server rather than embedding it in the URL. PAR + RAR is the recommended combination for financial-grade APIs.
The authorization_details value is what the AS will show in the consent UI — specific enough for the user to make an informed decision.
RFC 9396 §3: If authorization_details and scope are both present, both are applied. They are complementary, not mutually exclusive.

steps.label: GET /authorize (with authorization_details)
steps.explanation: The browser follows the redirect to the AS authorization endpoint. The AS parses the authorization_details, validates the payment_initiation type and its fields, and prepares a consent screen that shows the user exactly what they are authorizing — not just 'payments access' but the specific €250 payment.
annotations: RFC 9396 §5.1: The AS MUST validate the authorization_details parameter. Unknown types MUST be rejected with error=invalid_authorization_details.
The AS builds a consent UI that shows the user the specific authorization being requested — amount, recipient, account. This is far more informative than 'payments:write'.
RFC 9396 §5: The AS MAY downscope or modify authorization_details based on policy; the granted details are returned in the token response and embedded in the access token.

steps.label: POST /login (authenticate + approve payment)
steps.explanation: The user authenticates and approves the specific payment. The consent screen shows the exact amount (€250), recipient (John Smith), and IBAN — not a generic 'allow payments' prompt. The AS issues a code bound to these specific authorization_details.
annotations: The AS consent screen shows the specific payment details from authorization_details. This is the user's explicit, informed consent for this exact transaction.
Strong customer authentication (SCA) as required by PSD2 would be triggered here for payment initiation — typically requiring a second factor.
The authorization code is bound to the authorization_details that were requested; the AS will embed them in the access token on exchange.

steps.label: GET /callback?code=...&state=...
steps.explanation: The browser returns to the client callback with the authorization code. The client validates state and proceeds to exchange the code for an access token containing the specific authorization_details.
annotations: Standard state validation applies — verify the returned state matches the stored value before proceeding.
The code_verifier is retrieved from the server-side session for the PKCE verification in the token request.

steps.label: POST /token (code exchange)
Access token payload — authorization_details embedded
steps.value:
{
  "iss": "https://auth.example.com",
  "sub": "user_alice_482",
  "aud": "https://payments.example.com",
  "exp": 1700000300,
  "iat": 1700000000,
  "jti": "rar1a2b3c4",
  "client_id": "bankapp-client",
  "authorization_details": [
    {
      "type": "payment_initiation",
      "actions": ["initiate"],
      "locations": ["https://payments.example.com"],
      "instructedAmount": {
        "currency": "EUR",
        "amount": "250.00"
      },
      "creditorName": "John Smith",
      "creditorAccount": {
        "iban": "DE02100100109307118603"
      },
      "remittanceInformation": "Invoice #2024-0042"
    }
  ]
}

RFC 9396 §7: The AS MUST embed the granted authorization_details
in the access token. The RS uses this to enforce the exact
permitted operation — not just 'can make payments' but 'can make
exactly this €250 payment to this specific IBAN'.

Note: expires_in=300 (5 minutes) — payment tokens are typically
short-lived. There is usually no refresh token for one-time
payment initiation.

steps.explanation: The AS issues an access token with the authorization_details embedded directly in the payload. The token is not a generic 'payment access' credential — it is a cryptographically signed authorization for exactly one €250 payment to John Smith. The token response also echoes the authorization_details for the client's use.
annotations: RFC 9396 §7: The AS MUST include authorization_details in the access token. The RS is the enforcement point.
expires_in=300 (5 min) is typical for payment tokens — short-lived to minimize the window of misuse. No refresh token is issued for single-use operations.
The aud claim is scoped to the specific payments API location (https://payments.example.com) from the authorization_details.locations field.
RFC 9396 §5: The AS MAY grant a subset of the requested authorization_details. The client MUST check that the granted details are sufficient for the operation before proceeding.

steps.label: POST /payments (initiate payment)
steps.explanation: The client submits the payment request. The Payments API validates the access token and cross-checks every field in the request body against the authorization_details in the token. Amount, currency, IBAN, and recipient must all match exactly — any deviation is rejected.
annotations: RS enforcement: amount in request body (250.00 EUR) MUST match authorization_details.instructedAmount. A request for €500 would be rejected even with a valid token.
RS enforcement: creditorAccount.iban in request MUST match authorization_details.creditorAccount.iban. The token cannot be reused to pay a different recipient.
RS enforcement: actions=["initiate"] means the token can only initiate the payment, not cancel or modify it. Different actions require separate authorization.
This is the fundamental difference from scope-based access: the RS has cryptographic proof (via the signed token) of exactly what the user approved — not just a category of operations.
RAR pairs with PAR for high-security deployments: PAR pushes the full authorization_details server-to-server (no URL exposure), while RAR provides the fine-grained semantics.
